data.title: About 6 in 10 disapprove of Iran war: Pew polling
data.description:
A majority of Americans say they do not approve of the U.S. military action in Iran and that attacking the country late last month was the wrong idea, according to a poll released Thursday.

The Pew Research Center found 61 percent of respondents disapprove of President Trump’s handling of the conflict. This includes 44 percent who strongly disapprove and 17 percent who somewhat disapprove, with 37 percent who said they approve.

Another 59 percent of respondents said the U.S. made the wrong decision in using military force in Iran. Another 45 percent said the conflict is not going well for the U.S., with 28 percent who say it is going somewhat well. A quarter said the conflict is going extremely or very well.

Pew also found 90 percent of Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents disapprove of the conflict, with 88 percent calling the decision to strike wrong. Republicans mostly favor the conflict (69 percent) and the decision to attack Iran (71 percent).

Republican-leaning independents are more split on the war, with 52 percent approving of how Trump is handling it in contrast with 45 percent who disapprove.

Opinions are more split on the conflict’s future. Fifty-four percent say it will go on for at least six more months, 35 percent say it will go on for one to six months, 29 percent say it will last a year or more, and 8 percent say it will end in less than a month.

Most Democrats, at 68 percent, see the war stretching for six months or longer. That number is less among Republicans, where 58 percent say it will be over within the next six months.

Other polling has shown it has had a negative effect on Trump’s approval and disapproval ratings. Fox News released polling on Thursday showing his disapproval rating reached 59 percent, the president’s highest disapproval rating during both of his terms. Another 41 percent said they approve of Trump’s job performance.

The outlet found that 64 percent of registered voters disapprove of Trump is handling of the war with Iran. This also peaked well more than his highest disapproval ratings related to his policies toward Iran during his first term.

The Pew survey was conducted March 16-22 and included 3,524 respondents. The margin of error is 1.8 percentage points.

data.title: Trump says 'military wanted' White House ballroom: 'That was supposed to be secret'
data.description:
President Trump says there’s a national security component to the construction of the White House ballroom that was “supposed to be secret.”

“Now it’s no secret, the military wanted it more than anybody,” Trump said of the construction of the ballroom Thursday during a Cabinet meeting at the White House.

“It was supposed to be secret, but it became un-secret because of people that are really unpatriotic saying things,” he said.

“But doesn’t matter, it’s going to be great,” the president added.

Trump ordered the East Wing of the White House be demolished last year in order to construct a $400 million ballroom.

The president has frequently expressed the need for the 90,000-square-foot project, saying it’ll be utilized as a space for hosting state dinners, galas and other events with world leaders. The White House has said the ballroom is being funded by private donations. 

During the remodeling and expansion of the East Wing in the 1940s and following the attack on Pearl Harbor, then-President Franklin D. Roosevelt directed a bomb shelter be constructed underneath the building, according to the White House Historical Association. 

In January, CNN reported that the White House ballroom may include a “top-secret project” and that Trump was “rebuilding a secret bunker under the East Wing.”

The ballroom, which is expected to be completed by 2028, has triggered intense backlash from critics and faced significant legal challenges.

In December, the National Trust for Historic Preservation sued the Trump administration to stop construction of the ballroom until the White House obtained congressional approval and elicited a public comment period and multiple independent reviews.

“Everything I do, I get sued,” Trump said Thursday, pointing to the ballroom, as well as lawsuits involving the planned two-year closure of the Kennedy Center announced by Trump last month.

“I get sued over a ballroom that’s going to be the most beautiful ballroom in the country — so desperately needed,” Trump said.

data.title: Self-inflicted eclipse: How solar energy lost Republican support
data.description:
For decades, the solar deployment industry was a bipartisan darling — a rare status in Washington. The solar Investment tax credit was not a product of progressive activism but was created by a Republican trifecta in 2005, then extended repeatedly with overwhelming bipartisan support.

Back then, solar deployment incentives were viewed as a straightforward investment policy to mobilize private capital, expand electricity supply, and support domestic jobs. Solar power plants were not a cultural symbol. They were essential infrastructure.

Today, that coalition has largely collapsed — and with it, support for the solar deployment industry. 

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act sharply curtailed many incentives for solar deployment while safeguarding — even strengthening — programs designed to support high-value solar manufacturing that creates long-term, good-paying American jobs. The residential solar tax credit expired at the end of 2025, utility-scale projects face compressed eligibility windows, and new restrictions on rooftop leasing and third-party ownership threaten business models. 

These reversals did not happen overnight. They emerged gradually as trust eroded between the solar deployment industry and Republican policymakers.

Part of the breakdown stems from how solar was reframed in Washington. Democrats increasingly cast solar as a symbol of climate ambition and partisan loyalty, choosing to ignore the industry’s growing dependence on Chinese supply chains. Once solar became culturally owned by one party, polarization was inevitable. Unsurprisingly, Republican support for solar risked being interpreted as endorsing a broader climate agenda opposed by many conservatives.

Another factor is the solar trade associations, whose apparent goal is to maintain access to cheap Chinese panels regardless of the strategic cost. They have cried wolf repeatedly in response to policies addressing Chinese economic warfare and lawbreaking — and demand has never fallen off the cliff as predicted. That consistently pro-China playbook has left Republican policymakers fatigued and deeply distrustful.

Yet the underlying politics is more nuanced. Recent polling shows that Republican voters are not inherently hostile to solar. Support actually rises when projects use American-made technology with no ties to China. The lesson is clear: Republican skepticism is less about the technology itself than about the supply chains behind it.

The debate over Foreign Entity of Concern restrictions illustrates this dynamic. The widely held Republican view is that federal incentives should not subsidize Chinese jobs, particularly when supply chains intersect with national security. This position is not anti-solar. It is anti-dependence.

True to form, the solar deployment lobby resisted those restrictions, pressing for flexibility that Republicans interpret as, once again, protecting Chinese interests. Predictably, their positions reinforced the perception that solar deployment advocates want the benefits of federal subsidies without accepting the strategic constraints necessary to sustain them.

In Washington, credibility matters. Once policymakers conclude an industry doesn’t share their core objectives on national security and industrial resilience, no amount of rhetorical reframing easily restores it.

Instead of engaging those concerns directly, the industry’s response has been to double down on messaging — recruiting MAGA influencers and conservative media personalities to promote solar subsidies to Republican audiences, unconvincingly invoking themes like “energy dominance” and “America First.” But this tactic does little to address concerns and comes across as condescending and contrived.

If the solar deployment industry hopes to rebuild durable bipartisan support, it must stop trying to “convert” folks with rhetorical sleight of hand and instead, confront the issues Republicans consistently raise: supply-chain security, unfair trade practices and domestic manufacturing.

It should take a page from the playbook of America’s solar manufacturers — the genuinely American ones — who have demonstrated their commitment by investing billions in solar manufacturing and supply chains, creating tens of thousands of jobs, and ensuring supply security. Those companies are demonstrating that they’re America First through their actions, not just their words.     

Republicans are not inherently opposed to solar. They are opposed to policies that weaken American industry or deepen dependence on adversarial regimes.

Until the solar deployment industry engages those concerns seriously, the bipartisan coalition that once powered solar’s growth will remain in eclipse.

George David Banks is president and CEO of the American Council for Capital Formation. He has served as a senior energy policy adviser to Republicans in the White House and Congress.

data.title: Loomer: Zuckerberg addition to tech council 'slap in the face to MAGA'
data.description:
Conservative activist Laura Loomer on Wednesday said Mark Zuckerberg’s appointment to the inaugural President’s Council of Advisors on Science and Technology is a “slap in the face” to MAGA. 

“We went from Zuckerberg for Prison to Zuckerberg now being appointed to President Trump’s White House Tech panel. So much for accountability,” Loomer wrote in a post on the social platform X. 

“Zuckerberg deplatformed President Trump, censored his supporters and then helped steal an election. What a slap in the face to MAGA,” she added.

In the past, Loomer has harshly criticized Zuckerberg for banning Trump from Facebook following the Capitol riot and censoring content related to the assassination attempt on the president during the 2024 campaign. 

Trump also previously shared disdain for the Meta CEO, however, the tech giant has grown to become one of the president’s allies in the tech landscape. 

Zuckerberg attended Trump’s inauguration last year and Meta donated $1 million to his inaugural fund, alongside Amazon, Google and Boeing.   

Now, the Meta CEO will join Trump’s 13-member tech council and will be tasked with providing the president with information needed to inform public policy relating to the American economy, national and homeland security, employment and other topics, according to the White House. 

Zuckerberg will serve alongside Marc Andreessen, Sergey Brin, Safra Catz, Michael Dell, Jacob DeWitte, Fred Ehrsam, Larry Ellison, David Friedberg, Jensen Huang, John Martinis, Bob Mumgaard, and Lisa Su.

data.title: Hegseth prays at service for violence against 'those who deserve no mercy'
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth prayed at a Wednesday service in the Pentagon for violence against those “who deserve no mercy” and for god to “break the teeth of the ugodly.”

Hegseth read a prayer that he said was first delivered by a chaplain to troops ahead of an operation to capture Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro in January.

“Behold now the wicked who rise against your justice and the peace of the righteous. Snap the rod of the oppresor, frustrate the wicked plans and break the teeth of the ugodly. By the blast of your anger, let the evil parish,” Hegseth said at a monthly Christian worship service on Wednesday. 

Big Spring Sale

  • The top deals of the Amazon Big Spring Sale
  • Clearance sales under $50
  • Stock up on everyday essentials

BestReviews is reader-supported and may earn an affiliate commission.

“Grant this task force clear and righteous targets for violence. Surround them as a shield, protect the innocent and blameless in their midst, make their arrows like those of a skilled warrior who returned not empty handed. Let every round find its mark against the enemies of righteousness and our great nation,” he continued, speaking to uniformed military personnel and civilian employees.

“Preserve their lives, sharpen their resolve and let justice be executed swiftly and without remorse, that evil may be driven back and wicked souls delivered to the eternal damnation prepared for them.” 

The advocacy group Americans United for Separation of Church and State filed a lawsuit on Monday related to Hegseth’s monthly Christian worship services.

“Even if these prayer services are presented as voluntary, there is pressure on federal employees to attend in order to appease their bosses,” the group wrote.

Hegseth often invokes faith as the Pentagon head and is a part of the Communion of Reformed Evangelical Churches (CREC), a conservative network of Christian congregations. 

“And again to the American people, please pray for them every day on bended knee with your family, in your schools, in your churches, in the name of Jesus Christ. To the troops, keep going and Gvdspeed,” Hegseth said at the most recent press briefing at the Pentagon last week. 

The CREC network is co-founded by the self-described Christian nationalist Doug Wilson, who, along with other pastors, have preached at the Pentagon. 

Brooks Potteiger, pastor the Pilgrim Hill Reformed Fellowship in Tennessee, whose parishioners have included Hegseth, made news this week when he said James Talarico, a Democrat running for Senate in Texas, should be crucified.

Talarico responded to Potteiger in a statement, writing, “I love you more than you could ever hate me.”

Typically, U.S. military chaplains provide faith services at the Pentagon. 

On Tuesday, Hegseth announced two major changes to the group: they will no longer wear their rank insignia, but show insignia reflecting their religious affiliation and chaplains, who have been part of the military since 1775, will use 31 faith codes instead of over 200.

data.title: Chasing tourist dollars, a 'new and improved' Yucatan is boxing out its own people
data.description:
The edge of a tropical jungle is probably the last place — apart from a theme park ride — to find a sleek high-speed rail line. And yet El Tren Maya is just the latest example of how government-sponsored tourism has fundamentally transformed Mexico’s Yucatan peninsula. 

On one side of the tracks is dense, green foliage; on the other, not far from view, aqua blue Caribbean breakers gently roll in. As the train accelerates from the station in Cancun on a day trip to the colonial city of Valladolid, the scrub jungle passes in a blur. The ride is quiet and smooth, and on this weekday the train is largely empty. There is no Wi-Fi. Some trains have dining and sleeping cars and floor-to-ceiling windows. Station and train signage is in Spanish, English, and the Maya language.. 

Over the last 40 years, I have chronicled Cancun’s development into a glitzy resort on Mexico’s Caribbean coast. Millions of tourists travel there to enjoy cool, white, sandy beaches, snorkel at the many reefs and crawl the nightclubs, while staying at all-inclusive resorts. More serious visitors go inland to tour spectacular Maya ruins. To accommodate them, ruins have been cleaned and reconstructed. Nearby, rustic natural wonders like Xcaret have been Disneyfied into clean, almost antiseptic attractions. 

Nowhere is this boom more evident than in the peninsula’s transportation infrastructure. In 1994, an eight-lane tollway — the Supercarretera — was built in part to shovel Cancun’s tourists to the Chichen Itza Mayan ruins in less than two-and-a-half hours, shaving an hour off the pokey but endearing old highway meandering through interior Maya villages. At opening, the one-way, 160-mile trip from Cancun to Merida cost passenger vehicles $35.

Although Cancun’s airport has been enlarged several times, it can still take up to 90 minutes to clear immigration, collect luggage and pass through customs on busy, high season Saturdays. So, in 2023, a new airport opened with an adjoining executive aviation facility outside Tulum, 80 miles south. Upscale tourists can now avoid Cancun’s rowdy college spring breakers, families with children and unhip senior snowbirds, plus the resort’s endless chain restaurants and bars. 

Once a sleepy village adjoining the peninsula’s only seaside Maya ruins, Tulum has become a chic, “holistic hub” and New Age mecca. It features yoga in the jungle and beachside at sunrise, and the Temazcal, a traditional Mayan sweat lodge.

Still, in its first two years of operation, Tulum’s airport has struggled with fluctuating demand, due in part to higher-priced flights than into Cancun. 

Most recently, Mexico’s government invested in a $30 billion, thousand-mile high-speed rail line circling the entire Yucatan peninsula — a route hugging the coastal perimeter. It passes through five Mexican states. Former Mexican President Andrés Manuel López Obrador called it “the greatest construction project in the world.”

One tourist-related rail-line benefit is that it cuts travel time from Cancun to the Chichen Itza ruins to two hours, costing about $70 U.S. round-trip at current exchange rates. Another advantage: security. Tourists, who used to travel in rental cars can avoid extortion — sometimes at gunpoint in bogus traffic stops by police or police-impersonators.

Significantly, these huge capital projects starkly illustrate contrasting economic benefits distribution. The clear priority is tourism.

Yucatan is a major Mexican economy ATM. Although the Maya have benefited, their jobs have mainly been in construction and service sectors; staff from Mexico City and elsewhere hold most white-collar and supervisory jobs. 

Indigenous people built Cancun’s pyramid-shaped, beachfront resorts and the Riviera Maya to the south. Often, they used construction methods their Mayan ancestors probably used to build their magnificent cities and temples, utilizing native bamboo scaffolding. Their labor also helped build the superhighway, the Tulum airport and the rail line. 

Yet even with a sliding scale of fares for local and Mexican travelers, few subsistence farmers, whose land the government expropriated for these projects, can afford to drive the superhighway or ride the railway — hence all the empty seats. 

Environmental cost was high, as was local resistance. 

According to Conde Nast Traveler, Obrador’s government cut down as many as 9 million trees to build the railway, despite promising that he would cut down zero trees.

Mexican journalist and author Luis Hernández Navarro wrote in 2020 that the Tren Maya represented “savage capitalism that dispossesses the original peoples of lands and territories.” 

And last May, the Guardian quoted Indigenous leader Romel Gonzalez Diaz at a Xpujil village rally: “What is being done with the Maya train megaproject is not Mayan in any way.” 

The Mexican government shouldn’t underestimate this resistance. Rebellious Mayan peasants nearly drove their Mexican masters from Yucatan in the 1847-1915 Caste War.  

In Nelson Reed’s novel “The Cocom Codex,” a modern, rebellious young Maya lambastes a North American character: “This was our land … the Indian says, ‘Land of the Pheasant and the Deer, Land of the Rocks and Trees.’ Now we are marginalized, unimportant. Mexico City money builds a new city, Cancun, to entertain gringos, make a fortune, and what does the Maya get?” 

Mark I. Pinsky is a journalist and author based in Durham, N.C.

data.title: Trump says Iran's 'present' was oil ships through the Strait of Hormuz
data.description:
President Trump said Thursday that Iran’s “present” to the U.S. he mentioned two days earlier was the passage of 10 oil-carrying ships through the Strait of Hormuz.

Trump told reporters at the White House he was having “very substantial” talks with Iran as the conflict continues.

He said that during his discussion with Tehran, “they said ‘to show you the fact that we’re real and solid and we’re there, we’re going to let you have eight boats of oil.’”

“I didn’t think much about it, and then I watched the news, and they said…’There are eight boats that are going right up the middle of the Hormuz Strait,’” Trump added.

He said that as an apology, the nation also allowed two further boats.

DEVELOPING…

data.title: House Republican: Graham should 'have his Oval Office credentials revoked'
data.description:
Rep. Kat Cammack (R-Fla.) suggested Wednesday that Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) should have less access to President Trump as the conflict with Iran persists.

“I absolutely think he should have his Oval Office credentials revoked,” Cammack told Migrant Insider’s Pablo Manríquez as she departed the Capitol.

Graham, a staunch foreign policy hawk who has openly supported the U.S. military operation in the Middle East, is a frequent guest at the White House and often travels with Trump. On Monday, he and Sen. Bernie Moreno (R-Ohio) met with the president and newly minted Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin — before the latter was confirmed and sworn in — in the Oval Office. 

The South Carolina Republican also frequently travels to Israel. Earlier this month, he told the Wall Street Journal that before the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran, he met with members of Tel Aviv’s intelligence agency and coached Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu on how to convince Trump to go to war. 

Graham also strongly supported the January operation to capture former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro and has recently called for regime change in Cuba.

He even told host Shannon Bream on “Fox News Sunday” over the weekend that the U.S. should “take” Kharg Island, a critical oil depot for Iran.

“We did Iwo Jima. We can do this,” the senator said, referring to the 1945 battle that resulted in the deaths of more than 6,000 U.S. troops.

His rhetoric has garnered backlash from Democrats, as well as multiple House Republicans. 

Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), who opposes troops on the ground in Iran, wrote Wednesday on social platform X that “Washington’s war machine is hard at work. They are try to drag us into Iran to make it another Iraq. We can’t let them.”

Later in the day, Mace clarified, “And yes, when we say Washington’s war machine, we mean Lindsey Graham.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump says Iran 'begging to make a deal'; DHS shutdown grinds on
data.description:
President Trump is holding a Cabinet meeting on Thursday amid escalating tensions over the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown and war in Iran. It’s the first meeting for the new secretary of Homeland Security, Markwayne Mullin.

At the start of the meeting, Trump referenced reports of negotiations with Iran. He sought to clarify that Iran, not the U.S., is “begging” for a deal. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the Pentagon “would continue negotiating with bombs.”

Trump fired off several Truth Social posts Thursday morning, calling multiple times for an end to the filibuster, warning Iranian leaders that they “better get serious soon, before it is too late” in negotiations and trashing NATO for its lack of help against Iran. Iran’s leaders have been defiant in social media posts this week.

As the Iranian campaign nears its one-month mark, lawmakers from both parties are complaining about a lack of information from the administration regarding Operation Epic Fury.

The CPAC gathering in Texas will feature several speakers of note, including Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche, border czar Tom Homan and former Rep. Matt Gaetz (R-Fla.).

Follow live below.

data.title: NATO reports surge in Europe, Canada defense spending
data.description:
Europe and Canada had a 20 percent surge in defense spending in 2025 compared to the previous year, according to the annual report by NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, who said the alliance has “started a new chapter.” 

“We took big decisions and decisive action to defend the Alliance, protect our way of life, and keep one billion people safe on both sides of the Atlantic,” he wrote in the report’s foreword, which named Russia as the most significant and direct threat to NATO security.

But Moscow’s war in Ukraine, now in its fifth year, also is backed by China, North Korea, Iran and Belarus. Such threats require a continued momentum on defense spending, Rutte said.

“I expect Allies at the next NATO Summit in Ankara to show they are on ​a clear and credible path towards the 5% objective,” he wrote, ​adding that “a strong transatlantic bond remains essential in an age ⁠of global uncertainty.”

NATO leaders last year agreed to spend 5 percent of total GDP on defense and related investments ​by 2035. 

President Trump has repeatedly demanded that NATO countries ​significantly increase defense expenditures, criticizing some allies for not contributing their fair share to the group’s defense efforts and leaving countries like the U.S. to shoulder the majority of the cost. 

The Trump administration also maintains that European allies ​should eventually assume primary responsibility in defending the continent. The United States currently accounts for around 60 percent ​of alliance defense spending.

In addition, Trump in recent weeks has lambasted NATO allies in Europe for doing “absolutely nothing” to ​help with the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran.

Trump did not inform NATO allies prior to launching airstrikes on Iran at the end of February, though in mid-March he pressed alliance countries to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz – an ask many nations including Germany balked at.

“THE U.S.A. NEEDS NOTHING FROM NATO, BUT ‘NEVER FORGET’ THIS VERY ​IMPORTANT POINT IN TIME!” Trump wrote in a Truth Social post on Thursday.

In his report, Rutte said that last year “all Allies ‌reported ⁠defense expenditure figures that met or went beyond the 2% target first set in 2014, with many making steep increases in spending.”

Countries pledged to ​spend 3.5 percent of ⁠GDP on core defense, including troops and weapons, and 1.5 percent on broader defense-related investments such as cybersecurity, protecting critical infrastructure such as energy sources and adapting roads and bridges to handle ​heavy military ⁠vehicles, and strengthening defense industries.

Poland, Lithuania and Latvia have already hit the new 3.5 percent target last year, while several countries including Spain, Canada and ⁠Belgium, were ​at 2 percent, according to the report.

“This shows that NATO Allies recognize our changed security environment, and the need to meet our collective obligations,” Rutte wrote.

On average, the 32 ​NATO member countries spent 2.77 percent of GDP on defense in 2025, or more than $1.4 trillion combined. Of that, $574 billion came from Canada and Europe.
